mnemonics.title: SCARF - Steganography Requirements
mnemonics.content:
S - Security (undetectable without key)
C - Capacity (how much can be hidden)
A - Artifacts-free (no visible changes)
R - Robustness (resistant to attacks)
F - Fidelity (high quality stego-object)

mnemonics.title: LIONS - Steganography Types
mnemonics.content:
L - LSB (spatial domain)
I - Image steganography
O - OutGuess (tool for JPEG)
N - Noise insertion
S - Spread spectrum

acronyms.usage: The rightmost bit in a binary number; modifying LSBs doesn't significantly change the value, making it ideal for steganography
The leftmost bit in a binary number; changes to MSB significantly alter the value, not suitable for steganography
A transform used in JPEG compression; DCT-based steganography embeds data in frequency domain coefficients
A lossless image format commonly used for steganography as it preserves all pixel data (unlike JPEG)
An uncompressed image format ideal for spatial domain steganography due to its simple pixel structure

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a digital photo as a grid of colored dots. Each dot has RGB values like R=155, G=200, B=178. Binary: 10011011, 11001000, 10110010. Changing the last bit (LSB) from 1 to 0 changes R from 155 to 154 - virtually indistinguishable to the eye!

Think of a cover object as an envelope (cover letter) and the stego-object as the sealed envelope with hidden content inside. To an observer, it's just an envelope, but the recipient knows there's a message inside.

Imagine comparing two images side by side - one original, one with hidden data. Steganalysis放大s the differences (like using -20 brightness filter) to reveal patterns that normal eyes can't see. Statistical analysis looks for unusual byte patterns in the file structure.

analogies.analogy: If secret communication were a safe: Cryptography locks the safe with a combination that anyone can see is locked. Steganography hides the safe inside a pile of identical-looking empty boxes - you don't even know there's a safe to crack.

Think of pixel values like water levels in 1000 containers. Changing from 1000ml to 999ml is invisible to the eye. LSB steganography changes the last binary digit - from '1010' to '1011' - a difference of only 1 in a value of 10.

Imagine hiding a note in a book's index page (transform domain) vs in the text itself (spatial domain). Even if someone skims or summarizes the book (compression/processing), the index page reference remains intact.
